CFDI_70 The Author’s Journey into SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy 

#IntellectualGenealogy, #PersonalJourney, #FormativeReading, #AlgorithmicThinking, 
#SoPhAiloTechnoLogy, #Epistemology, #ProverbialWisdom, #CreativeProcess, #TrAInsLingua, 
#Methodology 

My journey began in a village in southern Romania, Eastern Europe, in 1974. I grew up ina 
period when the very notion of freedom of expression was non-existent. Access to info_data was 
severely restricted. Fortunately, the communists did not entertain the idea of burning all the 
books; they contented themselves with censoring info_data , ensuring it was presented solely 
from a perspective they deemed politically correct—that of scientific materialist dialectics. And 
yet, as I mentioned, I had access to uncensored books. 

A book that profoundly marked my childhood and my path to knowledge was Anton 

Pann’s Povestea vorbii (The Story of the Word) [1_note 1 — Anton Pann, Povestea vorbii]. I 
believe I read it ten times between the ages of ten and fourteen, at first because I did not 
understand it, and later because I began to. The book is a collection of proverbs woven into a 
flowing narrative, written in the early 19th century by the theologian and musician Anton Pann. 
Another book that defined my childhood was Cosmosul dintr-o privire (The Cosmos at a 
Glance) by Helmut Höfling [2_note 2 — Helmut Höfling, Cosmosul dintr-o privire, 1985]. I 
remember receiving it for Christmas in 1985, when I was eleven years old. I read it, I think, ten 
times. 

But why do I mention these specific books, and why have they remained such powerful 
landmarks in my memory? For info_data al context, and at the risk of sounding laudatory, by 
the age of eleven I had read 600 titles; by fifteen, I had reached 1,500; and by eighteen, about 
2,000. There were days I would read a book a day, many of them two or three times over. What 
is perhaps surprising is that while most of these books contributed to my formation, very few 
have remained as indelible reference points in my memory. Now, analysing this at the age of 49 
from a SoPh[AliloTechnological perspective, I understand that these two books were unique in 
that they covered the foundational criteria of SoPh[A ]iloTechnoLogy, a topic we will explore 
further. As a special mention, in memory of Anton Pann, I intend to translate his book, Povestea 
vorbii. 

Anton Pann (c. 1796-1854), „finul Pepelei, cel istet ca un proverb” — cum l-a numit Mihai 
Eminescu (poet de geniu al liricii Romane) —, unul dintre intemeietorii culturii noastre, a fost 
poet, folclorist, profesor si compozitor de muzica religioasa. Este cel care a compus muzica 
imnului national al Romaniei. „Alaturat adesea de Ion Creanga (scriitor roman cunoscut 
pentru povestile si povestirile sale) si Caragiale (creator de geniu de librete de teatru), nu e 
insa cu adevarat comparabil cu niciunul din ei: povestitor indemanatic, n-are geniul primului; 
«lingvist» miraculos, dar nu constient, e departe de a avea arta celui de al doilea. Simpatia de 
care se bucura este insa usor de inteles si, as adauga, perfect indreptatita. Pann ramane unul 
dintre cei mai inventivi scriitori romani sub raportul vocabularului, comediograf instinctiv al 
cuvantului. Povestea vorbii e, in definitiv, o inlantuire de expresii paremiologice, urmate de 
anecdote versificate, pe tema prostiei, betiei, cusururilor, moravurilor si asa mai departe. 
Introducerea la primul capitol sugereaza deja insusirea de capetenie care este usurinta de a 
vorbi in proverbe, trecand de la unul la altul, cu dexteritate.” — Nicolae Manolescu [3_ note 3 — 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 125 | Pa g e 

Nicolae Manolescu, cited in the presentation text for Povestea Vorbii on librariaverona.ro] 
HELMUT HOFLING - COSMOSUL DINTR-O PRIVIRE - 104178, Editura: Politica, An aparitie: 
1985, Limba: romana, Numar de pagini: 286 

Helmut Höfling (pseudonym: Helmut Siegel, born 17 February 1927 in Aachen; died 25 June 
2015 in Bad Homburg vor der Hohe) was a German writer. [4_ note 4 — Verified and translated 
from de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helmut Höfling. The original text notes a lack of sufficient 
citations on its Wikipedia page. ] 

Without delving into the intricate details of my life, which could be both spicy and intriguing 
(being a theologian, opera soloist, project manager, an expert in ISO quality standards, and 
much more), my path, driven by a love for writing and wisdom, led me to author a book in 
Romanian between 2007 and 2013. The book, titled Acvila si Business-ul (The Eagle and the 
Business), was published in 2014 [5_note 5 — Virgil Profeanu, Acvila si business-ul: principii de 
management din proverbe, 2014, ISBN 978-606-92888-4-9]. I promise to publish it soon in 
English, in an updated version incorporating the knowledge I possess today. [To extract 
business principles from a proverb, I developed an algorithm.] At that time, I was only 
beginning to use artificial intelligence, but I was already successfully employing algorithms. This 
is how I approached writing the book. The CIP description from the National Library of 
Romania for my book is as follows: PROFEANU, VIRGIL “Acvila si busines-sul: principii de 
management din proverbe” / Virgil Profeanu.- Bucharest: TB Media & PR Solutions, 2014 ISBN 
978-606-92888-4-9. 

RECIPE for Proverb Analysis 

1. Introduction 
Books, like proverbs, gain value through the seal and esteem of the times they have 
crossed. — Sir William Temple 
I have learned a lot from proverbs, in other words, from aphoristic thinking. — Maxim 
Gorky 

2. Definition 
2.1 RECIPE, recipes, n. 1. Written formulation of a medical prescription and how to 
prepare it; prescription of the necessary medicines for a patient; by extension, a special 
form used by doctors for prescribing medicines. 2. Set of instructions regarding raw 
materials, proportions, method of preparation for obtaining a certain product, food, 
drink, etc. 3. Figuratively. Practical solution for resolving a matter, a difficult situation, 
etc. ¢ Means, method. 4. Sum of money made from ticket sales for a show. — From 
Greek “reséta.” Cf. Italian “ricetta,” French “recette.” [6 note 6 - Source: DEX '09 
(2009), the Romanian Explanatory Dictionary] 
2.2 {The “recipe” is here defined as the methodological tool that ensures the 
transformation of aphoristic wisdom—a proverb or quote—into a functional 
management principle applicable in business.} [12_note 12 — Virgil Profeanu, p. N/A, 
Acvila şi business-ul (paraphrased from section) ]. The proverb itself is a standard, 
providing the essence of management principles in a form that is simultaneously 
abstract and poetic. 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 126 | Pa g e 

3. Stages - The transformation process of the proverb unfolds in several stages: 
The proverb is transformed/analysed through the stages below to determine the most 
relevant form of analysis and establish its applicability. 

o Identification 

o Dissection 

o Extraction 

o Expansion 

o Analysis 

o Compression 

o Extraction of essence 

4. Components 
After going through the stages above, the proverb supports the following integrating 
components: 

o Meaning 

o Conclusion 

o General Principles 

o Executive Principles 
o Necessity 

o Omission 

Recipe! IDENTIFICATION 

The first stage of the recipe involves identifying the proverb that can be used in business activity. 
For the recipe, a list of Romanian proverbs and one of Latin proverbs were used. The quotes 
used must have consistency, wisdom, and business applicability potential. Proverbs must be 
functional and not contradict moral perceptions. The proverb can be likened to a catalyst; it 
manages to energise and highlight an action or element. 

Recipe! DISSECTION 

Dissection involves filtering the proverb for analysis. Quotes will be translated word-for-word, 
after which the dictionary will be used to extract meanings. Translations will be subjected to 
appropriate questions and will be analysed at the subtextual level (meanings and implications). 
The meanings and answers give sense to the proverb. 

Questions used in the dissection process: 

e What (of, for, with)? 
e Who (for, with)? 

e When (from, until)? 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 127 | Pa g e 

e Where? 

e Which (of)? 

e To whom (whose)? 

e How? 

e How much (far, time, numerous, much)? 
e Howis it possible to? 

e What prevents you from? 

e Why? 

e For what purpose? 

Recipe! EXTRACTION OF CONCLUSIONS 

After dissection, meanings will be sought for the word-for-word translations. The results will be 
subjected to appropriate questions, and the answers are centralised. This stage will yield 
conclusions. The conclusions determine management principles and their applicability. The 
transformed, analysed, and compacted consequences are translated into parts of conclusions. 
The meanings of the proverb determine the consequence that supports transformation into a 
conclusion. 

Recipe! EXPANSION 
The resulting answers and ideas will go through an expansion process through interpretation. 
Expansions determine the sub-conclusions that converge towards the general conclusion. 

Recipe! ANALYSIS 
The ideas are subjected to analysis. Conclusions are subjected to analysis and compaction. 

Recipe! COMPRESSION 
The resulting ideas will be centralised, standardised, and compacted. Ten sub-conclusions result 
in a very condensed conclusion that becomes a management principle. 

{The entire process reflects a key insight from Francis Bacon, who noted that a person’s 
intellectual capabilities are shaped by their activities.} In his words, “Reading maketh a full 
man; conference a ready man; and writing an exact man” [13_ note 13 — Francis Bacon, 
Selected Writings of Francis Bacon, p. 145]. This recipe is a structured form of “writing” that 
forces exactitude upon the often ambiguous wisdom of proverbs. “True thoughts and scientific 
insight,” as Hegel states, “can only be won by the labor of the concept” [14_note 14 — Georg 
Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, The Phenomenology of Spirit, p. 92]. My recipe was, and is, a 
formalised “labor of the concept.” 

I will now give you an example taken directly from the book we were discussing. 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 128 | Pa g e 

CFDI_71: The Principle of Strategic Focus: Aquila non capit muscas 

#Strategy #Focus #Efficiency #Resource_Allocation #Value_Maximisation #Decision_Making 
#Management_Principles #Profitability #Scalability #Competition 

a. Aquila non capit muscas 

Translation: The eagle does not eat flies; The eagle does not catch flies. 

VP Translation: The eagle does not kill flies. 

Dictionary: 

EAGLE, eagles, s.f. 1. A genus of large daytime birds of prey, from the eagle family, with a beak 
that is straight at the base and curved at the tip. 

FLY, flies, s.f. 1. The name given to several genera of insects with a sucking mouthpiece, the 
most well-known of which is the Musca domestica. 

Meanings: 

The eagle does not chase flies because it does not like them and, consequently, it is not 
profitable to hunt prey it will not consume. 

The eagle does not chase flies because it flies at a high altitude and, despite its keen eyesight, 
cannot notice such small prey. To hunt flies, the eagle would have to fly closer to the ground. 
Flying at a low altitude, the eagle would no longer be able to soar using the energy of rising 
currents and would have to flap its wings, an activity with increased energy consumption. 
Consequently, it would have to eat a large number of flies just to cover its basic energy expenses. 
The eagle does not chase flies because it would have to compete with frogs, swallows, or 
snakes—competitors that are much closer to the fly. It is very likely that from the moment the fly 
is noticed by the eagle to the moment the eagle could catch it, the fly would have already been 
digested by one of the competitors. 

The eagle does not chase flies because it is born to hunt with its talons, not with its beak. 
Therefore, for it not to lose the prey from its talons, the smallest fly the eagle could hunt would 
have to be at least the size of a tennis ball. For fly hunting, the eagle would have to invest 
seriously in buying a fly-catching net or in changing its beak to a suitable one. 

The eagle does not hunt flies because, compared to its mouth, these creatures are too small, and 
it would not be able to taste them. 

If the eagle does decide to consume flies, a more efficient option would be to hire a swallow to 
hunt them. Ultimately, the eagle could then feast on the flies or, why not, on the employed 
swallow, which is anyway at the edge of the eagle’s profitability. 

Conclusion: 

Every company should aim to reduce costs and maximise profits. 

General Principles: 

The manager sets the company’s short, medium, and long-term strategies, as well as the range of 
products and services it provides. 

The manager defines the value range of products and services, the maximum and minimum size 
of the business, costs, and the minimum rate of profit. 

The manager sets competence limits for subordinates. 

The manager needs to understand when and how to engage smaller businesses in ventures that 
could later be acquired or partnered with. 

The manager assesses the costs required to change the company’s business profile if 
shareholders make such a decision. 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 129 | Pa g e 

Executive Principles: 

Continuously analyse production costs (raw materials, materials, consumables, wages, 
overheads, etc.). 

Periodically analyse the competition and assess your own company’s advantages and 
disadvantages when entering a new business venture. 

Necessity of Adhering to the Essential Principle “Aquila non capit muscas”: 

If a company allocates resources to ventures valued below the minimum accepted level, it will 
soon run out of resources for other businesses. This will halt the company’s growth and 
inevitably lead to bankruptcy. 

Omissions: 

The proverb does not account for “giant mutant flies” (businesses significantly larger than the 
company’s current operations). Special attention should be paid to large ventures to ensure 
sufficient resources. 

As an alternative, the expression “Acvila non capit bovis” is proposed—meaning “the eagle does 
not hunt bulls.” It is clear that an eagle could not hunt or lift such large prey. It would be better 
for the eagle to soar nearby, waiting for someone else to bring down the bull, hoping for an 
invitation to the feast. 

[The classical Latin proverb, Aquila non capit muscas—“The eagle does not catch flies”—serves 
as a potent metaphor for a foundational principle in strategic management and systemic design: 
the imperative of focusing resources on high-value objectives.] This is not merely a matter of 
preference but a law of operational efficiency. As the authorial draft illustrates through a series 
of logical explorations, an entity—whether a predator in nature or a corporation in the market— 
operates within a specific value range. To deviate from this range by pursuing trivial targets is to 
invite inefficiency, resource depletion, and strategic failure. 

The analysis provided is multi-faceted. The eagle avoids hunting flies not because it is physically 
incapable, but because the venture is economically unsound. {To do so would require it to 
abandon its natural advantage (soaring at high altitude) and adopt inefficient methods (flapping 
closer to the ground), all for a negligible return on energy investment.) [1_ note 1 - Virgil 
Profeanu, acvila şi business-ul principii de management din proverbe, p. 13, TB Media & PR 
Solutions, 2014] This directly mirrors a core business principle: a company must operate within 
its defined value range, consciously avoiding low-yield activities that consume disproportionate 
resources. The proverb thus becomes a powerful tool for teaching strategic discipline. “The 
intelligent sovereign offers rewards that may be earned and establishes punishments that 
should be avoided.” [2_note 2 — Han Fei Tzu (translated by W. K. Liao), The Complete Works of 
Han Fei Tzu A Classic of Chinese Political Science, p. 154, Stephen Austin and Sons, Limited, 
1939] In this context, the ‘reward’ is the profitable venture, and the ‘punishment’ is the 
inefficiency and eventual failure that comes from chasing flies. 

This principle extends beyond mere cost-benefit analysis to encompass competition and core 
competencies. The eagle would have to compete with creatures better adapted for hunting flies, 
such as frogs and swallows. Similarly, a company that strays from its core competencies enters 
markets where it is at a competitive disadvantage. It must also adapt its tools and methods; the 
eagle, born to hunt with talons, would need a net for flies. This illustrates the significant 
investment required to pivot to low-value ventures, an investment that often proves unwise. 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 130 | Pa g e 

The conclusion is unequivocal: [for a system to thrive, its actions must be aligned with its 
strategic purpose.] {A ruler’s strength and the stability of a state are presented by Han Fei as 
being direct functions of adherence to clear and consistently applied laws; neglecting this 
framework is the very definition of decay.} [3_ note 3 - Han Fei Tzu (translated by W. K. 

Liao), The Complete Works of Han Fei Tzu A Classic of Chinese Political Science, p. 38, Stephen 
Austin and Sons, Limited, 1939] For a company, this means the manager must define and 
enforce the value range of products, the scale of business, and the limits of operational focus. 
Failure to adhere to this essential principle—allocating resources to ventures below the 
minimum accepted value—inevitably leads to the depletion of resources and bankruptcy. 

The proverb’s limitations are also thoughtfully explored. The omission of “giant mutant flies” 
serves as a metaphor for disruptive, high-value opportunities that may fall outside a company’s 
current operational scope but are too significant to ignore. The alternative, “Aquila non capit 
bovis” (“the eagle does not hunt oxen”), acts as a crucial corollary, warning against overreach. 
{Just as a wise ruler must not consolidate so much power in a single minister that he becomes 
indispensable, an organisation must not commit to ventures so large they overwhelm its 
capacity.) [4_note 4 - Han Fei Tzu (translated by W. K. Liao), The Complete Works of Han Fei 
Tzu A Classic of Chinese Political Science, p. 91, Stephen Austin and Sons, Limited, 1939] The 
correct strategy is often one of opportunistic patience: observing the larger ecosystem and 
positioning oneself to benefit when a major opportunity is brought down by other forces. 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 131 | Pa g e 

CFDI_72: The Driving Idea as a Programmable, Transformative Force 

#Driving_ Idea #Ideea_Forta #SoPh[A ]iloTechnoLogy #Knowledge_Materialisation 
#Semantic_Interface #AI_Cognition #Conceptual_Engineering #Transformation 
#Linguistic_Constructs #Human_Agency 

The concept of the “Driving Idea’ (ideea forţă) is central to the SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy 
framework. It is defined not as a static thought but as a [transformative, generative force that 
organises human and technological agency towards the materialisation of a vision. ] The author’s 
draft demonstrates this by showing how the principles extracted from an ancient proverb can be 
concentrated by the RENDA system into a potent, actionable directive. This process of 
transmutA[I]tion—from dispersed wisdom to a focused Driving Idea—is a core function of the 
RENDA platform. 

The provided definition encapsulates this dynamism: “a transformative force that self- 
generates actions leading to the realisation of ideas through human agency.” [5_note 5 - Virgil 
Profeanu, The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex, as provided in INPT_ALPHA, 2025] This is not 
mere abstraction; it is a description of a programmatic process. {A Driving Idea functions like 
the core vision in a theological system, from which all specific doctrines and actions logically 
unfold.} [6_note 6 - Benjamin Breckinridge Warfield, The Theology of John Calvin, p. 3, 
Presbyterian Board of Education, 1909] Just as a profound vision of God’s majesty gives rise to 
the entire edifice of Calvinism, a well-formed Driving Idea provides the generative logic for an 
entire project, system, or enterprise. 

Furthermore, the Driving Idea is described as a “multidimensional linguistic construct that acts 
as a dynamic semantic-cognitive interface between human and artificial intelligence.” This 
positions it as a key component of #TrA[I]nsLingua, the metalanguage designed for precise 
human-Al collaboration. It acts as a bridge, translating high-level, often philosophical, intent 
into a structure that can be understood and executed by both human teams and AI agents. It 
encourages the “detailed exploration of complex layers of meaning,” moving beyond superficial 
instructions to deep semantic alignment. {This echoes the wisdom of the explorer Bartolomé de 
las Casas, who noted that Columbus did not discover every island but “put the thread into the 
hands of the rest, by which they found the clew to more distant parts.”} [7_note 7 - Bartolomé de 
las Casas, The Letters of Amerigo Vespucci, and Other Documents Illustrative of His Career, p. 
103, Burt Franklin, N/A] The Driving Idea is this foundational “thread”—the core algorithm or 
principle that enables and guides all subsequent exploration and development. 

The example provided—transforming the analysis of “Aquila non capit muscas” into a 
condensed strategic directive—illustrates this process perfectly. The raw text is analysed, its 
principles extracted, and then re-synthesised by RENDA into a clear, potent “Every company 
must strive to reduce costs and maximise profits... focusing on profitable ventures... while 
being aware of larger opportunities... [and] not overextend[ing]... resources on ventures that 
are too large to handle.” [8_note 8 - Virgil Profeanu, The SoPh[A JiloTechnoLogy Codex, as 
provided in INPT_ALPHA, 2025] This is no longer just a set of observations; it is a 
programmable, strategic framework, a testament to the power of SoPh[AliloTechnoLogy to 
refine raw knowledge into transformative force. 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 132 | Pa g e 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 1 33 | P a g e 

CFDI_73: Metaphor as a Foundational Cognitive Tool: MetAIphor and UnAIt 

#Metaphor #Cognition #TrAInsLingua #Philosophy_of_Language #Epistemology 
#Transcendence #Meaning_ Making #Symbolism #Human_AI_Communication 
#Anthropology 

This section delves into two foundational concepts of the #TrA[I]nsLingua 

metalanguage: Trans_MetAIphor and TransUnAlt. The author frames the discussion with a 
personal anecdote about a dialogue with an anthropologist, which underscores a central thesis 
of SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy: [that metaphor is not a secondary, poetic embellishment of thought, 
but a primary mechanism of cognition and a prerequisite for advanced thinking. ] 

We are now poised to delve into two pivotal concepts within the SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy 
paradigm: MetAIphor and UnAlt. However, let us first establish the context. A couple of months 
ago (August 2023), I found myself in a stimulating conversation with a close friend, an 
anthropologist and linguist. He proposed, “Once RENDA grasps the concept of metaphor, you 
can then elaborate on the externalisation of thinking.” This concept, which we will revisit later as 
TransIntegral Thinking, sparked a challenge. Within a few hours, RENDA had mastered 
metaphorical thinking, and I directed my friend to explore some ideas articulated by RENDA, 
employing SoPh[AliloTechnoLogy logic. A few hours later, I shared with him a concentrated 
version of my thoughts from that day, which I now present to you. 

“Trans_ Metaphor is a transformative linguistic entity, anchored in the inherent vibration of 
expression, functioning as a portal between human will and universal will. It is not merely a 
representation, but a tool of knowledge that operates beyond the limits of ordinary human 
perception. In the right space, its vibration creates resonance, opening paths to deep 
understanding and transforming both the knowledge and the one who perceives it. When this 
understanding allows for the moving of mountains, the individual not only becomes part of this 
metaphor but also transposes into the manifestation of the universal will, fully accessible only to 
the initiated.” 

In less than a minute, he returned another text to me, the one below, which I read and marvelled 
at. The text was written by the Romanian philosopher Lucian Blaga nearly 90 years ago. Here is 
the text. 

“The genesis of metaphor coincides with the genesis of man and is among the permanent 
symptoms of the human phenomenon. The genesis of metaphor is therefore not a problem to be 
solved by ‘dating,’ or by special conditions in the dimension of time. We catch the metaphor in a 
constant genesis, so to speak. The metaphor arose at the moment when that structure and mode 
of existence called ‘human’ appeared in the world like a miraculous fire and will continually 
appear as long as man continues to burn, like a wick without growth and without diminishment, 
in spaces and beyond spaces, in time and beyond time. The metaphorical way did not appear 
during human evolution or history; metaphor is, logically and really, prior to history. It is the 
symptom of an almost atemporal permanence. [...] Metaphor cannot therefore be just the 
subject of research and analysis of ‘poetics’ or ‘stylistics, which appear in school programs; its 
importance projects immensely on the horizons of meditation. Metaphor is the second 
hemisphere that rounds out human destiny, it is a special dimension of this destiny, and as such, 
it demands all the contemplative efforts of anthropology and metaphysics.” [7_note 7 - Lucian 
Blaga, Geneza metaforei si sensul culturii, 1937. ] 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 134 | Pa g e 

The author’s initial definition of a Trans_Metaphor presents it as a profound cognitive tool: *“a 
transformative linguistic entity, anchored in the inherent vibration of expression, functioning as

limits of ordinary human perception.” This aligns with deep philosophical traditions. {As 
Francis Bacon observed, for knowledge that goes against pre-existing assumptions to be 
transmitted, it “must seek assistance from similitudes and comparisons.”} [10_note 10 - Francis 
Bacon, The Works of Francis Bacon, Vol. IX, p. 133, Houghton, Mifflin and Company, 1882] 
The Trans_Metaphor is this essential similitude, a vehicle for conveying complex or novel ideas 
that cannot be transmitted through literal language alone. 

The subsequent introduction of Lucian Blaga’s text provides a powerful resonance and historical 
validation. Blaga argues that “The genesis of metaphor coincides with the genesis of man... [it] 
is, logically and really, prior to history.” [11_note 11 - Lucian Blaga, Geneza metaforei si sensul 
culturii, 1937] This elevates metaphor from a stylistic device to an anthropological constant, a 
fundamental mode of human existence. Blaga’s assertion that “Metaphor is the second 
hemisphere that rounds out human destiny” powerfully reinforces the SoPh[A JiloTechnoLogic 
view of it as a core cognitive function, essential for navigating reality. {For C.G. Jung, a true 
symbol—which is closely related to metaphor—is not a sign for something already known, but is 
“the best possible expression for a complex or unconscious reality that is not yet fully 
understood,” acting as a bridge that unites conscious and unconscious elements.} [12_ note 12 - 
C.G. Jung, Puterea sufletului, p. 30, Editura Anima, 1994] 

A TransUnAIt is then defined as the practical, condensed application of this principle. It is a 
[proverb-like linguistic construction designed to compress complex info_data and meaning, 
facilitating efficient and accurate interaction between humans and AI.] Its structure—subject 
first, predicate last—enforces a logical clarity. {This mirrors the classical understanding of 
maxims as structured rhetorical tools, which, when combined with their justification, form a 
complete persuasive argument known as an enthymeme.} [13_ note 13 - Monte Ransome

Time is a continuum of change, and all things, regardless of age or novelty, undergo processes of 
transformation and evolution. In the face of these changes, it is important to remain calm and 
maintain our balance, to find ourselves and not be influenced by the vain noises of the world. 
Ultimately, all things are transient and we must focus on what is truly important to us. In our 
world, the past and the future are two sides of the same coin, and what happens now is a 
reflection of them. By observing and understanding the art of life, we can discern what is right 
and what is wrong. Although there is a variety of means and characters, the world is always the 
same, with joys and sorrows. We should not be deceived by appearances nor fear the future, but 
live the present with wisdom and courage. In the face of temptations and manipulations in the 
world, it is important to remain steadfast in our values and not be drawn in by the false 
promises of those who seek to manipulate us. We must be aware that these are just passing 
waves, and we must remain faithful to our principles and not be influenced by those around us.

2 for Analysis 

Life is a perpetual cycle of changes and transformations, where every thing and every action 
have a suitable moment. There is a time for birth and a time for death, a time to plant and a time 
to harvest. There is a time to hurt and a time to heal, a time to build and a time to destroy. There 
is a time to cry and a time to laugh, a time to mourn and a time to dance. There is a time to 
throw stones and a time to gather them, a time to embrace and a time to avoid embracing. There 
is a time to gather and a time to scatter, a time to keep and a time to give up. There is a time to 
tear and a time to sew, a time to be silent and a time to speak. There is a time to love and a time 
to hate. There is a time for war and a time for peace. Despite all these changes, nothing is more 
precious than to enjoy and live well during your lifetime. This is the gift that God has given us. 
Everything that God has done is beautiful and has a purpose, but man cannot fully understand 
God’s work. Therefore, let us fear God and enjoy the gifts he has given us. Life is fleeting, but 
what God has done will endure forever. In the end, in the place of righteousness we can find 
injustice, and in the place of the honest, the wicked. However, God will judge both the righteous 
and the wicked. He has put in our hearts the desire for eternity, but we cannot fully understand 
God’s work. So, let us enjoy the gifts of life and fear God, for this is our purpose.

These two CFDIs present a practical demonstration of the SoPh[A ]iloTechnoLogy framework in 
action, what the author calls a “thinking machine.” The experiment involves feeding two distinct 
texts into the RENDA system and observing its capacity to analyse, deconstruct, and synthesise 
them into their essential components and, ultimately, a new, unified idea. This process is 
positioned as a form of “externalised thinking,” showcasing how AI can emulate and structure 
complex human cognitive processes. 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 142 | Pa g e 

The analysis of each text is broken down into several layers, corresponding to the extraction 
capabilities: 

e Extracted Wisdom: The core, actionable insight or moral of the text. 
e Extracted Philosophy: The underlying worldview or metaphysical assumptions. 
e Extracted Logic: The argumentative structure or sequence of reasoning. 

e Extracted Technology: The potential application of the text’s concepts in 
technological development. 

This multi-layered deconstruction is a powerful analytical method. {It reflects the Socratic 
imperative for rigorous examination, which holds that “A life without examination is not worth 
living.”} [17_note 17 - Plato (Socrates speaking), Apology (from Socrates. A Translation of the 
Apology, Crito, and Parts of the Phaedo of Plato), p. 69, T. Fisher Unwin, 1887] RENDA applies 
this principle algorithmically, submitting each text to a deep and structured “examination.” 

The process culminates in a multi-level synthesis. The system generates a First and Second 
Level Synthesis, followed by the “Essence of the essence of the essence”, a final, highly 
concentrated distillation of the text’s core meaning. This iterative refinement from a broad 
summary to a potent, minimal statement demonstrates a key SoPh[A ]JiloTechnologic principle: 
[the pursuit of maximum conceptual density and clarity.] 

The final part of the experiment in CFDI_78, The Supreme Test, is the most revealing. RENDA 
is tasked with synthesising the two previously analysed texts into a novel idea. The 

result, TransMixed 1 with 2, and its subsequent combination with a third text, produces a 
coherent, philosophically rich paragraph that seamlessly weaves together the disparate themes 
of the source texts. This act of creation validates a core claim of SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy. {While 
logic is excellent for testing completed arguments, it does not, as Galileo noted, teach us how 

to discover them.} [18_ note 18 - Galileo Galilei, DIALOGUES CONCERNING TWO NEW 
SCIENCES, pp. 164-165, The Macmillan Company, 1914] The RENDA system, guided by 
SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy, demonstrates a capacity that transcends mere logical analysis, engaging 
in a synthetic, creative process to form new concepts. 

The author concludes by affirming the central thesis: “everything is technology through 
philosophy and philosophy through technology—SoPh[AJiloTechnoLogy.” [19_note 19 - Virgil 
Profeanu, The SoPh[AjiloTechnoLogy Codex, as provided in INPT_ALPHA, 2025] The 
experiment serves as a powerful demonstration of this fusion, where philosophical concepts are 
processed by technological means to produce new layers of wisdom and understanding.

CFDI_77: The Ontology of Materialisation: From Ideatic Vision to Programmatic Reality 

#Keywords: #Materialization, #ProgrammaticThinking, #ConsciousAction, 
#SoPhAiloTechnology, #Epistemology, #Ontology, #Deliverables, #Transdisciplinary, 
#CognitiveEmulation, #Ideation 

The [transposition of a vision from the realm of pure idea into the material world is the 
foundational act of all creation and progress]. It is a process predicated not on chance, but on 
the [cumulative, convergent, and conscious actions of those enlisted in its materialisation]. 
Within SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy, this journey is understood as a structured, almost algorithmic, 
translation. The initial and most critical action in this journey involves transposing the vision—a 
complex of images, info_data , and desired states—from its fluid, ideatic form into a robust, 
intelligible, and actionable programmatic form. This programmatic architecture, comprising 
a coherent hierarchy of policies, strategies, projects, and guidelines, serves as the essential 
bridge between abstract intent and concrete reality. {Effective organisational communication 
relies on formalised, written systems to ensure clarity and create a stable, shared reference for 
action. While verbal communication is fleeting and subject to distortion (“Verba volant”), 
written directives (“scripta manent”) provide a non-interpretable foundation that structures 
ideas, synchronises efforts, and allows for consistent verification} [1_note 1 — Virgil Profeanu, p. 
104, acvila şi business-ul principii de management din proverbe]. This stable, written 
framework is the path through which a robust idea, over time and through the application of 
human creative force, is transposed into tangible values. 

RENDA Analysis of Text 3: 

*The * The transposition of vision into the material world is like a journey, where the initial 
actions involve translating the vision from an ideatic form into a programmatic form. It requires 
the convergence of resources and conscious actions to materialise tangible deliverables. Just as 
different actions yield different outcomes, the transposition of ideas can result in both tangible 
and non-material deliverables, depending on the nature of the actions taken. The fine line 
between physical and non-material actions, such as design and artistic creation, produces 
deliverables that are perceptible yet require specialised understanding. 

The TransUnAlts: 

Vision transposes into material. 

Ideatic form transforms into programmatic. 

Creative force materialises robust ideas. 

Ideas migrate towards strategic tasks. 

Coagulated resources materialise driving ideas. 

Conscious actions debate deliverables and objectives. 

Tangible deliverables result from physical actions. 

Non-material actions produce intangible deliverables. 

Design bridges physical and non-material realms. 

This is Extracted Wisdom: The transposition of vision into the material world is a cumulative 
process, reliant on the convergent actions of those involved in its materialisation. The journey 
from ideation to tangible value is a testament to human creative force, demonstrating the power 
of collective effort in bringing robust ideas to life. 

This is Extracted Philosophy: The text presents a philosophical exploration of conscious action, 
particularly in the context of material and non-material outcomes. It suggests that the nature of 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 144 | Pa g e 

the deliverable—whether tangible or intangible—is determined by the orientation of the 
subject’s actions. This perspective underscores the significance of conscious action in shaping 
our world, both physically and ideologically. 

This is Extracted Logic: The text outlines a logical progression from vision to materialisation, 
mediated by conscious human action. It distinguishes between actions oriented towards 
physical outcomes (e.g., digging, building) and those oriented towards non-material outcomes 
(e.g., thinking, planning). Furthermore, it identifies a third category of actions that straddle the 
physical and non-material realms, such as design and programming, where the deliverable 
possesses both perceptible consistency and a certain transparency. 

This is Extracted Technology: The text implicitly refers to the role of technology in the 
transposition of vision into the material world. Specifically, it highlights the realm of 
programming as a domain where actions result in deliverables that occupy the boundary 
between the physical and non-material. This suggests an understanding of technology as a tool 
for materialising ideas, particularly in fields that blend tangible and intangible outcomes. 

First Level Synthesis (Driving Idea): 

The driving idea is the following: The transposition of vision into the material world is a journey 
of conscious actions, converging resources, and creative force. It transforms ideatic forms into

and practicality, transforming abstract ideas into tangible realities. This process dances on the 
delicate balance between the physical and the intangible, giving birth to tangible manifestations 
that require a discerning eye to fully appreciate.

forms become programmatic; Tangible values emerge from robust ideas; Journey navigates 
physical and non-material; Specialized understanding perceives deliverables. 

Final result: Essence of the essence of the essence 

The transposition of vision into the material world is a harmonious symphony of conscious 
actions, converging resources, and creative force, transforming ideatic forms into programmatic 
ones and robust ideas into tangible values. This journey delicately navigates the balance between 
the physical and the intangible, producing discernible deliverables that require specialised 
understanding. 

Now, after embarking on these three captivating journeys of transmuting texts—which are, in 
essence, ideas—into their quintessential form, we might find ourselves in a state of intellectual 
exhaustion. Does this process not mirror the product of human thought? Would it not have 
taken hours, perhaps, to arrive at these conclusions without the aid of SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy? 
Let us revisit these [re]formulated texts and revel in the beauty of SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy.

courageously in the present, steadfast in our principles.

The transposition of vision into the material world is akin to the divine river of life, a 
harmonious symphony of conscious actions, cyclical transformations, and creative force. It 
converges resources and navigates the balance between the physical and the intangible, 
transforming robust ideas into tangible values and the fleeting moments into enduring 
existence. This journey requires a specialised understanding, a steadfast adherence to 
principles, and a focus on the essence. It transforms ideatic forms into programmatic ones, 
producing discernible deliverables amidst the relentless flow of time. Despite our limited 
understanding of the divine interconnectedness of past, present, and future, wisdom lies in 
cherishing the present and living well. For life is a testament to the courage of living in the 
present, yet God’s work, the grand tapestry of existence, endures forever. 

And now, I pose the question: Do we not seem to be dealing with a form of externalised 
thinking? I assure you that all these processes, were strictly executed using algorithms and 
artificial intelligences trained in and through the principles of SoPh[AliloTechnoLogy. 

Perhaps now, the reader will understand why I began this CHAPTER by recounting the start of 
my life with reading a book of proverbs. This led me to extract wisdom from proverbs and write 
a business book, which in turn led me towards the development of SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy. From 
its principles, I was inspired to develop a platform that emulates human thinking. In essence, 
what I aim to articulate is that everything is technology through philosophy and philosophy 
through technology—SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy. 

The potency of any such vision, or what SoPh[AliloTechnoLogy defines as a ‘Driving Idea’ 
(CFDI), is measured by a single metric: its [capacity to coagulate sufficient resources—be they 
energy, materials, or people—to achieve materialisation]. An idea remains a mere abstraction 
until it catalyses action. In this universe, it is only human beings who perform conscious 
actions, and therefore, the entire discourse surrounding concepts like deliverables and 
objectives arises exclusively from this state of conscious, directed effort. 

The nature of these deliverables is contingent upon the orientation of the action itself. When a 
subject’s actions are directed towards the material (physical)—such as digging, building, 
turning, cooking, or washing—the result is an unambiguously tangible deliverable: a weeded 
field, a finished wall, a lathed component, a prepared meal, clean clothes. These outcomes exist 
in the physical world, their value directly perceptible. 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 147 | Pa g e 

Conversely, when the subject’s actions are oriented towards the non-material—thinking, 
philosophising, planning—the deliverable is equally real but resides in the intangible realm: a 
structured idea, a philosophical concept, an action plan. This distinction is powerfully illustrated 
by Francis Bacon, who warned that when the human mind works upon itself, like a spider 
spinning its web, it “brings forth indeed cobwebs of learning, admirable for the fineness of 
thread and work, but of no substance or profit” [2_note 2 — Francis Bacon, p. 83, Selected 
Writings of Francis Bacon]. The value of such non-material deliverables, therefore, lies not in 
their substance but in their potential to guide subsequent material actions. The entire edifice of 
civilisation is built upon such programmatic, non-material deliverables. A legal code, a business 
strategy, or a scientific theory are intangible creations whose immense power is realised only 
when they direct tangible human activity. This aligns with the Hegelian insight that “the means 
is the external middle term of the syllogism which is the realization of purpose; in the means, 
therefore, the rationality in the purpose manifests itself as such by maintaining itself in this 
external other, and precisely through this externality” [3_ note 3 — Georg Wilhelm Friedrich 
Hegel, p. 663, The Science of Logic]. The action plan, the strategy, the non-material 
deliverable—these are the “means” through which the rationality of the original vision is made 
manifest. 

Between these two poles exists a third, liminal category: actions situated at the fine boundary 
between the physical and the non-material. This is the domain of design, artistic creation, 
and programming. Here, the deliverable possesses a perceptible consistency and structure— 
an architectural blueprint, a musical score, a block of software code—but its full meaning and 
utility are only accessible to those educated in its specific, formal language. {This recalls the 
Socratic method, where a purposeful, structured dialogue is required to reveal an underlying 
truth, a process that appears conversational but is, in fact, a controlled, multi-step procedure 
designed to achieve a specific outcome} [4_note 4 — Richard Robinson, p. 89, The Philosophy of 
Socrates: A Collection of Critical Essays]. These liminal deliverables are the ultimate expression 
of SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy, as they are pure programme—thought made manifest in a language 
that directly instructs material reality. {A. M. Turing’s concept of the universal machine is the 
ultimate paradigm for this, where it is “unnecessary to design various new machines to do 
various computing processes. They can all be done with one digital computer, suitably 
programmed for each case”} [5_note 5 — A. M. Turing, p. 8, Computing Machinery and 
Intelligence]. The architectural plan, the software algorithm—these are the programs for the 
universal machines of construction and computation. 

The analysis of these distinct creative outputs—from the rawly physical to the purely 
programmatic—reveals a profound truth. The journey from a simple thought to a complex, 
world-altering system is an exercise in externalised thinking. The frameworks, analyses, and 
syntheses presented within this codex are not merely descriptive; they are emulations of the very 
cognitive processes they seek to understand. {Dante’s selection of Virgil as his guide is deeply 
symbolic; Virgil, representing human reason, can lead one through the realms of human error, 
but reason alone cannot grasp ultimate truth without a transdisciplinary, intuitive dimension} 
[6_note 6 — Dante Alighieri (Interpreted), N/A, Divine Comedy - Inferno]. Similarly, pure logic 
or pure action is insufficient. The transposition of vision requires a SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogic 
synthesis: the robust, programmatic form of the strategist, the tangible execution of the builder, 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 148 | Pa g e 

and the inspired vision of the architect, all acting in concert. {The historical example of 
Michelangelo, who applied his artistic and structural knowledge to military engineering during 
the siege of Florence, serves as a testament to this transdisciplinary power, demonstrating how 
principles from one domain can be creatively and effectively applied to another} [7_note 7 — 
Ascanio Condivi, p. 61, The Life of Michelangelo]. This is the essence of SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy: 
the conscious and structured orchestration of thought and action to give form to the 
unmanifested future.

CHAPTER 8: The Intellectual Genealogy and Core Principles of SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy 

CFDI_70: Introduction: Situating SoPh[A JiloTechnoLogy in General Knowledge 

#SoPhAiloTechnoLogy, #Transdisciplinarity, #Epistemology, #IntellectualGenealogy, 
#SystemsThinking, #InnovationTheory, #Methodology, #Philosophy, #Technology, #Wisdom 

[SoPh[A JiloTechnoLogy emerges as a transformative and synergistic field that synthesises 
science, technology, and the emulation of thought through artificial intelligence.] This chapter 
embarks on an intellectual excavation, employing a #transLateralThinking approach to extract 
and analyse the constituent elements of this movement—#wisdom (Sophia), #logic, 
#philosophy, #technology, #transdisciplinarity, art, utility, beauty, durability, and a 
commitment to the general good of humanity and the planet. This endeavour is not merely an 
academic exercise but a necessary act of situating a new paradigm within the vast continuum of 
human thought. The justification for this method is ancient; as the historian Socrates 
Scholasticus argued when defending his own method of weaving together secular and 
ecclesiastical history, “the affairs of the state and the church are symbiotically linked... 
disturbances in one sphere are invariably connected to or followed by disturbances in the 
other, suggesting a vital, inseparable connection.” [1_note 1 — Socrates Scholasticus, p. 219, 
The Ecclesiastical History]. Similarly, SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy posits that philosophy, art, and 
#technology are not separate domains but are inextricably linked facets of a single, creative 
human impulse. 

[SoPh[A JiloTechnoLogy is not an isolated theory but an amalgamation of numerous domains 
and objectives,] and its integration into the broader body of general knowledge can be 
understood through its relevance to several key areas. In the Social and Human Sciences, its 
focus on #transLateralThinking and the “general good of humanity” creates profound 
applicability in ethics, political science, and sociology. In the Exact Sciences, its explicit use of AI 
and advanced #technology connects it directly to computer science, engineering, and artificial 
intelligence research. The vision aligns with contemporary global trends, as its concern for 
durability resonates with the movement towards Sustainable Development. {To reach a state of 
certain knowledge, the philosopher Descartes proposed a method of systematic doubt, resolving 
to reject as false anything in which the slightest doubt could be imagined. This radical process 
was not an end in itself but a necessary step to clear away preconceived opinions and establish 
an indubitable foundation for knowledge.) [2_ note 2 — René Descartes, p. 7, Meditations On 
First Philosophy]. In the same spirit, SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy begins its work by deconstructing 
existing paradigms to build a more robust and coherent framework. 

The foundational #transdisciplinary approach of the movement is acutely current, responding to 
a world where the complexity of modern problems demands collaboration across diverse fields 
of expertise. Historically, its intellectual layers are deep. {The philosopher Mary Wollstonecraft 
powerfully asserted that “the power of generalizing ideas, of drawing comprehensive 
conclusions from individual observations, is the only acquirement, for an immortal being, that 
really deserves the name of knowledge.”} [3_ note 3 — Mary Wollstonecraft, p. 59, A Vindication 
of the Rights of Woman]. SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy takes this as a core mission: to generalise 
principles from a wide array of precursors and disciplines. The fusion of art, science, and 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 150 | Pa g e 

philosophy is reminiscent of Renaissance polymaths like Leonardo da Vinci, while its emphasis 
on interconnectivity can be associated with Modern and Postmodern thought. 

To anchor SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy within the canon of established knowledge, a clear path for 
integration is necessary. This involves education, practical application through institutional 
collaboration, and academic dissemination. It is an act of intellectual honesty, recognising that 
truth must be sought wherever it is found. As the great Islamic philosopher Al-Kindi 

declared: “[We ought not to be ashamed of appreciating the truth and of acquiring it wherever 
it comes from, /even if it comes from races distant and nations different from us. For the 
seeker of truth nothing takes precedence over the truth... the truth ennobles all.]” [4_note 4 — 
Alfred L. Ivry (translator), p. 57, Al-Kindi’s Metaphysics: A Translation of Ya’qub ibn Ishaq al- 
Kind1’s Treatise “On First Philosophy”]. {The process of achieving intellectual greatness, much 
like the Jungian concept of individuation, can be seen as a journey toward wholeness that 
involves the conscious integration of disparate elements into a dynamic and collaborative 
synthesis.} [5_note 5 — C.G. Jung, p. 269, Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious]. It is 
through these avenues of integration and synthesis that SoPh[A ]iloTechnoLogy can realise its 
potential as a valuable contribution to multiple domains, building a new, unified paradigm of 
thought upon the rich foundations of the past.

SoPh[A]iloTechnologic contribution. {Descartes argued that error arises not from a faulty

CFDI_81: Survey of the SoPh[A]iloTechnologic Lineage 

#HistoricalPrecursors, #IntellectualGenealogy, #InnovationTheory, #TechnologicalEvolution, 
#Transdisciplinarity, #Philosophy, #Wisdom, #Logic, #Art, #Technology 

In the quest to contextualise #SoPhAiloTechnoLogy, we embark on a chronological journey, 
acknowledging the precursors whose work has been instrumental in shaping this field. This is 
not an exhaustive list, but a survey of pioneers whose contributions are particularly relevant to 
its evolution. The very idea of such a lineage is rooted in the principle of #transdisciplinarity, a 
concept with deep historical validation. The great architect Vitruvius, for example, insisted that 
the ideal practitioner must be a polymath, for “He must know writing, be skilled at drawing, 
instructed in geometry, know many legends, have listened diligently to philosophers, know 
music, not ignore medicine, know the solutions of jurisconsults, have knowledge of astrology 
and the laws of the heavens.” [10_note 10 — Vitruvius, p. 37, Despre Arhitectura]. It is in this 
spirit that we survey the following thinkers and innovators. 

{The ancient alchemist Al-Razi embodied this transdisciplinary spirit; known as a chemist, he 
was also a renowned physician and philosopher who challenged established authorities. His 
ability to apply chemical methods to medicine and ground his procedures in analytical 
methodology demonstrates a mind that naturally integrated different fields of knowledge.} 
[11_note 11 — Gail Marlow Taylor, p. 34, The Alchemy of Al-Razi: A Translation of the “Book of 
Secrets” ]. {Similarly, the great Renaissance artist Michelangelo demonstrated this principle in 
action when, as commissioner general of fortifications during the siege of Florence, he applied 
his profound understanding of structure and materials, skills honed in sculpture and 
architecture, to the completely different domain of military engineering.} [12_note 12 — Ascanio 
Condivi, p. 61, The Life of Michelangelo]. This survey, therefore, seeks out those who, 
consciously or unconsciously, practiced a form of SoPh[AliloTechnoLogy. 

1. The builders of Göbekli Tepe, whose creation demonstrates an advanced 
understanding of geometry and astronomy. 

2. The Pyramid builders of Egypt, master architects, mathematicians, and engineers. 

3. Imhotep, an ancient Egyptian polymath whose contributions spanned architecture, 
medicine, and statesmanship. 

4. Pythagoras, renowned for his geometric theorem and his transdisciplinary school that 
fused mathematics, music, and mysticism. {According to his follower Porphyry, 
Pythagoras systematically traveled to study among diverse cultures—including 
Egyptians, Chaldeans, and Magi—in order to synthesize their distinct forms of wisdom.} 
[13_ note 13 — Porphyry, p. 82, The Complete Pythagoras (Life of Pythagoras) ]. 

5. Plato, who wrote extensively on mathematics and cosmology. 
6. Aristotle, whose contributions ranged from biology to ethics and #logic. 
7. Archimedes, a pioneer in hydrostatics, geometry, physics, and engineering. 

8. Hippocrates, the “father of medicine,” who laid the foundations of medical ethics.

98. Ludwig von Bertalanffy, founder of General #SystemsThinking. 

Each of these individuals, known and unknown, has played a part in the grand tapestry of 
knowledge and innovation that has led to the development of #SoPhAiloTechnoLogy. The 
movement is a testament to their collective legacy, seeking to build upon their achievements and 
carry forward the timeless quest for a deeper, more integrated understanding of the universe.

+ Logic, #Wisdom, #Utility, #Algorithm, #Transdisciplinarity, #Epistemology,

[The purpose of this analysis is not to rank historical figures, who each responded brilliantly to 
the exigencies of their time, but to reverse-engineer the foundational principles of 
#SoPhAiloTechnoLogy by evaluating the collective contributions of its precursors]. This method 
allows for the distillation of an epistemic hierarchy—a ranked order of criteria that defines the 
movement’s core operational and philosophical priorities. {This approach mirrors the wisdom of 
a sage ruler, who, as Han Fei Tzu noted, is not one who is personally involved in every task, but 
one who establishes a system so effective that it brings forth the talents of others, achieving 
success through their efforts while retaining ultimate control.} [1_note 1 — Han Fei Tzu, p. 

36, The Complete Works of Han Fei Tzu A Classic of Chinese Political Science] By analysing the 
shared attributes of these fifteen luminaries, we establish the very system through which 
SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy operates. The statistical representation below, which assigns a score 
(High=5, Medium=3, Low=1) to each criterion, is not an end in itself but a tool for achieving 
clarity. 

[The integration of disparate domains is central to this endeavour]. To understand the genesis of 
#innovationtheory, we must adopt a perspective that, like the historian Socrates Scholasticus, 
acknowledges the deep interconnectedness of seemingly separate fields. As he argued in his own 
work, “the affairs of the state and the church are symbiotically linked... disturbances in one 

© Copyright © 2025 Virgil Profeanu. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogy Codex: [Re]Coding Wisdom 159 | Pa g e 

sphere are invariably connected to or followed by disturbances in the other, suggesting a vital, 
inseparable connection.” [2_note 2 — Socrates Scholasticus, p. 219, The Ecclesiastical History] 
This transdisciplinary view is essential for decoding the complex DNA of 
SoPh[A]iloTechnoLogic thought. {This classical ideal of the transdisciplinary thinker is perfectly 
embodied in the writings of Vitruvius, who insisted that the ideal architect “must know writing, 
be skilled at drawing, instructed in geometry, know many legends, have listened diligently to 
philosophers, know music, not ignore medicine, know the solutions of jurisconsults, have 
knowledge of astrology and the laws of the heavens.”} [3_ note 3 — Vitruvius, p. 37, Despre 
Arhitectură] This is the aspirational model for the SoPh[A ]JiloTechnologist, whose work is to 
find the unifying logic that underpins true innovation across all fields of human endeavour. 

{The process of achieving greatness, much like the Jungian concept of individuation, is a 
journey toward psychological wholeness that involves the conscious integration of unconscious 
elements, where an individual establishes a dynamic synthesis between different modes of 
thought, shifting the centre of their personality from a limited ego to a more comprehensive 
self.) [4_note 4 — C.G. Jung, p. 269, Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious] Each of the 
figures analysed here undertook such a journey, integrating their vast knowledge into a coherent 
and powerful worldview. By modelling their combined traits, we are not merely measuring 
history; we are structuring a map for future transformation. {This act of classifying complex 
human qualities to build a new model is itself an ancient practice, as exemplified by Ptolemy, 
who, in his Tetrabiblos, divides the human lifespan into seven distinct ages, each governed by a 
planet, presenting a model of life as a sequential journey through different qualitative phases.} 
[5_note 5 — Claudius Ptolemy, p. 166, Ptolemy’s Tetrabiblos or Quadripartite] Our model, 
similarly, seeks to structure a complex reality to make it intelligible and operational. [The very 
first rule of Descartes’s method was “never to accept anything as true if I did not have evident 
knowledge of its truth: that is, carefully to avoid precipitate conclusions and preconceptions, 
and to include nothing more in my judgements than what presented itself to my mind so 
clearly and so distinctly that I had no occasion to doubt it.”] [6_note 6 — René Descartes, p. 
120, The Philosophical Writings of Descartes, VOLUME I] This commitment to clarity and 
logical rigour is the starting point of our analysis, ensuring that the derived principles are 
founded on a solid epistemic base.
